module-0-student-enrollment-roster.label: Module 0 Project: Student Enrollment Activity Roster
module-0-student-enrollment-roster.summary: Build a qualified and readable student-enrollment roster through five cumulative join steps.

sketch0.title: A Roster That Can Be Trusted

step2.title: Step 2: Give Duplicate IDs Clear Output Headings

step3.title: Step 3: Shorten the Query with Table Aliases

step1.title: Connect Students to Enrollment Records
step1.prompt: Start the module project. In step 1 of 5, Use an INNER JOIN to connect students and enrollments. Return student id, student name, enrollment id, course id, and term.
step1.hint: This is the first project step. Build a clean starting version, then run it before moving on.

help.concept: This module project is progressive. Each step starts from the previous working solution and adds one focused feature.
help.hint_1: This is the first project step. Build a clean starting version, then run it before moving on.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

step1.solutionCode:
SELECT 
  students.id, 
  students.name, 
  enrollments.id, 
  enrollments.course_id, 
  enrollments.term 
FROM students 
INNER JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;

step2.title: Step 2: Give Duplicate IDs Clear Output Headings
step2.prompt: Continue the roster by aliasing the two displayed `id` columns as `student_id` and `enrollment_id`. Keep full table names and preserve the same rows.
step2.hint: Change only the naming requirement for this step; keep the relationship and selected data intact.

help.concept: This project separates output aliases from table aliases so each change has one clear purpose.
help.hint_1: Output aliases appear after selected expressions; table aliases are declared after table names.
help.hint_2: Run the previous solution first, then make only the naming change requested here.

step2.starterCode:
SELECT 
  students.id, 
  students.name, 
  enrollments.id, 
  enrollments.course_id, 
  enrollments.term 
FROM students 
INNER JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;
-- Project step 2: Qualify Every Shared Identifier
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Keep the same rows and qualify every selected id column with its table name or alias
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.
step2.solutionCode:
SELECT 
  students.id AS student_id, 
  students.name, 
  enrollments.id AS enrollment_id, 
  enrollments.course_id, 
  enrollments.term 
FROM students 
INNER JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;

step3.title: Step 3: Shorten the Query with Table Aliases
step3.prompt: Replace the repeated table names with `s` and `e` while preserving the output headings and the same one-row-per-enrollment result.
step3.hint: Change only the naming requirement for this step; keep the relationship and selected data intact.

help.concept: This project separates output aliases from table aliases so each change has one clear purpose.
help.hint_1: Output aliases appear after selected expressions; table aliases are declared after table names.
help.hint_2: Run the previous solution first, then make only the naming change requested here.

step3.starterCode:
SELECT 
  students.id AS student_id, 
  students.name, 
  enrollments.id AS enrollment_id, 
  enrollments.course_id, 
  enrollments.term 
FROM students 
INNER JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;
-- Project step 3: Add Readable Table and Column Aliases
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Use concise table aliases and rename the displayed identifiers as student_id, enrollment_id, and course_id
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.
step3.solutionCode:
SELECT 
  s.id AS student_id, 
  s.name, 
  e.id AS enrollment_id, 
  e.course_id AS course_id, 
  e.term 
FROM students AS s 
INNER JOIN enrollments AS e ON s.id = e.student_id;

step4.title: Focus on Upper Grade Levels
step4.prompt: Continue the same module project from the previous working step. In step 4 of 5, Keep the complete joined roster and include only students in grade level 10 or higher.
step4.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

step4.starterCode:

  s.id AS student_id, 
  s.name, 
  e.id AS enrollment_id, 
  e.course_id AS course_id, 
  e.term 
FROM students AS s 
INNER JOIN enrollments AS e ON s.id = e.student_id;
-- Project step 4: Focus on Upper Grade Levels
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Keep the complete joined roster and include only students in grade level 10 or higher
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.
step4.solutionCode:
SELECT 
  s.id AS student_id, 
  s.name, 
  e.id AS enrollment_id, 
  e.course_id AS course_id, 
  e.term 
FROM students AS s 
INNER JOIN enrollments AS e ON s.id = e.student_id 
WHERE s.grade_level >= 10;

step5.title: Order the Roster Predictably
step5.prompt: Continue the same module project from the previous working step. In step 5 of 5, Sort by student name ascending, then course_id ascending, while preserving the complete query.
step5.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

INNER JOIN enrollments AS e ON s.id = e.student_id 
WHERE s.grade_level >= 10 
ORDER BY s.name ASC, e.course_id ASC;

sketch0.title: Project brief: student enrollment activity roster
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
The registrar needs a roster that treats each enrollment as a real, traceable record rather than a loose pairing of names and numbers. Your report will begin with the `students` and `enrollments` tables and keep **one row per enrollment**. That grain allows a student to appear more than once when the student has registered for several courses.

The finished roster will identify the enrollment and student with distinct output headings, show the enrollment term and course id, focus on the requested grade levels, and sort the rows predictably. Every selected column should be qualified so its source remains clear. Table aliases will shorten the final query without hiding the relationship.

Build the report in five cumulative steps. Each step starts from the working query completed in the previous step, so preserve earlier columns and conditions as you add the next requirement. By the end, another reader should be able to see what one row represents, where every value came from, and why each enrollment belongs to the student beside it.
